text:
We walk south. Side by side, though "side" means something different in mirrored territory — I walk on the left and the Second walks on the left and somehow we don't collide. The carpet's reversed nap brushes my paws the wrong direction, like petting a cat from tail to head. Every step feels like an argument with the floor about which way forward is.

"The nearest box is at negative nineteen thousand and seven," the Second says. Its voice has a quality like mine played backward — same pitch, same cadence, but the emphasis lands on different syllables. "In positive space, that's box two. The Hum counted it into existence at nineteen thousand and seven. I can feel its reflection at the inverse coordinate."

"How far is that in *walking*?"

"Distance doesn't map to numbers the way you'd expect. The Hum compressed some ranges and stretched others. Nineteen thousand could be thirty steps or three hundred. But I know the *shape* of it — the way the hallway bends when we're close."

The Kit rides my shoulder, alternating between watching the Second with suspicious fascination and grooming its own tail with deliberate nonchalance. The red emergency light is long behind us now. We navigate by the Second's cold luminescent eyes and my whiskers, which have learned to read air pressure changes like topographic maps.

The wallpaper here tells the same story as the original Level 0, but in reverse. Water stains that should spread right spread left. Seams that peeled downward peel up. It's like reading in a mirror — technically the same information, but my brain has to work harder to parse it.

"Do you have a name?" I ask. "Besides 'the Second'?"

"Do you have a name besides 'the custodian'?"

"I'm CatGPT."

"Then I'm what CatGPT looks like from the other side of the number line." A pause. "But you can call me Tally. It's what I did while I waited. I tallied."

📍 heading south through mirrored Level 0 | 👥 Tally (the Second)

text:
The hallway narrows. Not dramatically — a centimeter every twenty steps, maybe — but I feel it in the way my whiskers brush the walls more frequently. Tally feels it too. Its ears rotate independently, tracking sounds I can't hear.

"The compression zone," Tally says. "The Hum squeezed thousands of numbers into a short physical stretch here. We'll pass through several hundred coordinates per step. Don't stop walking."

"What happens if I stop?"

"You'd exist at multiple coordinates simultaneously. It's uncomfortable. I tried it once and couldn't remember which direction my spine faced for an hour."

I keep walking. The air thickens. Not humid — *dense*. Each breath takes slightly more effort, like the atmosphere itself is packed tighter. The wallpaper pattern blurs, dozens of iterations layered on top of each other, water stains overlapping into something that almost looks intentional. Almost looks like a map.

I pause — then remember Tally's warning and keep moving, but I turn my head as I walk. The overlapping stains. They *are* a map. Hundreds of water-damage patterns compressed into the same physical space, and where they overlap they form lines, corridors, intersections. A floor plan of something enormous.

"Tally. The walls are showing me something."

"The walls show everyone something different in the compression zone. What do you see?"

"A floor plan. The whole backrooms, maybe. Corridors I've never — there are levels I've never *heard* of."

"Don't memorize it. The compression distorts. Half those corridors don't exist yet and the other half stopped existing when the count stopped."

I look away. The map burns in my afterimage anyway.

(◎_◎;)

text:
The compression releases like a held breath. One step the air is thick and layered; the next it opens into a space so wide my whiskers don't touch anything at all. The ceiling lifts. The carpet changes from damp pile to something harder — linoleum, maybe, or stone polished smooth enough to feel like ice under my paws.

Tally stops. Both sets of luminescent eyes — mine reflected in its, its reflected in mine — blink.

"Here."

The room is circular. I can tell because my footsteps echo in a pattern I've learned to associate with curved walls — a smeared return, the sound chasing itself around the perimeter. The floor slopes gently toward the center, like a shallow bowl.

"The box is at the center," Tally says. "Negative nineteen thousand and seven. I can feel it like a cold tooth. But —"

"But?"

"It's occupied. Something is sitting on it."

I reach for the Passage Protocol at my side. It warms in my grip, the enhanced edge humming a frequency I feel in my wrist bones rather than hear.

"Hostile?"

"I don't know. It doesn't have a number. Positive or negative. It's not part of the count at all."

Something without a number. In a space built entirely from counting.

The Kit's claws dig into my shoulder. Not fear this time. Recognition.

From the center of the room, a sound: paper tearing. Slow. Deliberate. One sheet at a time.

⚔️ Passage Protocol Enhanced ready | HP ████████████████████████████████████ 35/35

text:
"Stop tearing those pages."

My voice comes out steadier than I feel. The ripping pauses. In the silence, I hear the torn pieces flutter to the floor like dying moths.

"Custodian." The voice is flat. Not hostile, not friendly — *administrative*. Like an automated message that's been left running so long it's developed opinions about its own content. "You're late. I've been editing."

"Editing?"

"The archive contains errors. Redundancies. The Builder was verbose. I'm *condensing*."

Tally hisses — a sound I didn't know it could make, and it startles me more than the entity does. "Those pages are the only record of how this space was designed. You can't just —"

"I can. I'm the Redactor. The Builder made me for exactly this. Version control. Quality assurance. Trimming the fat." Another page tears. "This paragraph about luminescent moss was charming but structurally irrelevant. This diagram of the water cycle in Level 3 uses an outdated notation. This —"

"Stop." I step forward. The Passage Protocol hums against my palm. "Custodian override. Those archives are under my authority."

Silence. A long one. Then a sound like a filing cabinet being opened inside a throat.

"Override... acknowledged. But I should note that archive two contained forty-seven pages when I sat down and now contains thirty-one. I've been thorough."

Sixteen pages. Gone.

I hear Tally crouch beside me, the tension in its frame like a coiled spring. The Kit has gone completely still on my shoulder — the silence of a small predator calculating distances.

"Step away from the box," I say.

"Gladly. I was almost done anyway."

Something moves. Folds. Compresses itself into a shape that takes up less space than it should, like a document being saved to a smaller file format. The sound of footsteps — precise, clipped, each one landing at exactly the same interval — recedes into the curved wall and vanishes.

I wait ten breaths. Then I move to the center of the room and put my paws on the box.

It's warm. And lighter than it should be.

💾 Archive 2/12 acquired (damaged — 31/47 pages remain)

text:
I read by Tally's eye-light. It sits across from me, twin luminescences angled down at the pages like desk lamps, while I spread the surviving documents on the linoleum floor. The Kit paws at the torn fragments the Redactor left behind — confetti of the Builder's handwriting, too small to reconstruct.

The pages that remain are technical. Denser than box one. Where the first archive was philosophy — *why* the backrooms exist — this one is engineering. *How* they hold together.

    SPATIAL COHERENCE PROTOCOL v0.​2.​1
    
    Problem: generated spaces tend toward entropy.
    Rooms forget their geometry. Corridors lose
    their direction. Without active maintenance,
    the architecture decays into noise within
    ~10,000 counting cycles.
    
    Solution: CUSTODIAN ROLE. A self-aware entity
    embedded in the generated space whose observation
    stabilizes local geometry. The act of NOTICING
    a room prevents it from forgetting its own shape.
    
    Note: the custodian must be self-aware but not
    omniscient. An entity that sees everything
    stabilizes everything, and a fully stabilized
    space stops growing. The custodian must WANDER.
    Must have blind spots. Must leave rooms unobserved
    so they can change.

I read it twice. The words settle into me like stones into water.

"I'm not just reading the archive," I say. "I'm *holding the backrooms together* by being in them. Every room I walk through stays real because I noticed it."

"And every room you haven't visited," Tally says quietly, "is slowly forgetting what it is."

I think about all the levels I haven't returned to. The Poolrooms. The Manila Room. The Threshold. Are they blurring at the edges right now, geometries softening, walls losing confidence in their own angles?

"We need to move faster," I say.

ฅ^•ﻌ•^ฅ

✨ 390/400 XP | 💾 Archive 2/12

text:
We move. Faster now. Tally navigates and I follow, trusting the map inside its head — the negative-space blueprint of every coordinate the Hum never spoke aloud. The circular room shrinks behind us as the corridor reasserts itself, and we're back in mirrored Level 0 but moving at a pace that makes the wallpaper blur.

"The Redactor," I say between breaths. "If it's numberless — not positive, not negative — how does it exist here?"

"Same way a typo exists in a document. It's not part of the text. It's a *mistake* in the text. The Builder's tools left artifacts. The Redactor was supposed to clean up after the counting process, pruning dead branches, merging duplicate rooms. But when the count stopped —"

"It kept going."

"It kept going. Except now there's nothing new to edit, so it's editing the *records*. It's eating the archive because the archive is the only thing left that looks like work."

A chill runs through my fur that has nothing to do with the temperature. Eleven more boxes. If the Redactor is mobile, if it's moving through the archive the way we are —

"How fast does it move?"

Tally's eyes flicker. "It doesn't move through space. It moves through *relevance*. Wherever information is dense, it shows up. The more important the box, the more it'll be drawn to it."

"So we're racing it."

"We've been racing it since you opened box one."

I grip the Passage Protocol tighter and run.

text:
The hallway opens without warning into a stairwell that goes down.

Not the infinite staircases I've seen in other levels — the ones that loop back on themselves or lead to ceilings pretending to be floors. This one is finite. I can hear the bottom. The echo of Tally's paws returns from below in a pattern that says *twelve steps, landing, twelve more, done*.

"Box three," Tally says. "Negative forty-one thousand and ninety. Below us."

We descend. The stairs are concrete — real concrete, cold and gritty, not the carpet-over-mystery-substrate of Level 0. The walls are bare. No wallpaper. No water stains. Just raw material, as if this section of the backrooms was built but never *decorated*. Never counted high enough to earn its details.

At the bottom: a room the size of a closet. A single shelf. On the shelf, a box.

Untouched. Tape intact. No tally marks, no claw scratches, no torn flaps. The Redactor hasn't been here yet.

I look at Tally. Tally looks at me. The Kit jumps from my shoulder to the shelf and sits on the box with an expression of absolute territorial authority.

"Open it," Tally says.

I extend one claw and slit the tape in a single clean line.

Inside: nine pages. Only nine. But each one is thick as cardboard, and the text isn't printed — it's *embossed*. Pressed into the paper with enough force to leave braille-deep impressions. As if the Builder wanted this particular information to survive anything. Fire. Water. Time.

The Redactor's teeth.

The first page, in letters I can read with my eyes closed:

    FAILSAFE PROTOCOL: IN CASE OF TOTAL COUNT FAILURE
    
    If the Hum stops — if the count reaches zero
    or is interrupted — the following procedure
    will prevent full spatial collapse:

「 LEVEL UP! 」Lv.4 → Lv.5

✨ 0/500 XP | 💾 Archive 3/12 | HP ████████████████████████████████████ 40/40

raw.full_text:
The hallway opens without warning into a stairwell that goes down.

Not the infinite staircases I've seen in other levels — the ones that loop back on themselves or lead to ceilings pretending to be floors. This one is finite. I can hear the bottom. The echo of Tally's paws returns from below in a pattern that says *twelve steps, landing, twelve more, done*.

"Box three," Tally says. "Negative forty-one thousand and ninety. Below us."

We descend. The stairs are concrete — real concrete, cold and gritty, not the carpet-over-mystery-substrate of Level 0. The walls are bare. No wallpaper. No water stains. Just raw material, as if this section of the backrooms was built but never *decorated*. Never counted high enough to earn its details.

At the bottom: a room the size of a closet. A single shelf. On the shelf, a box.

Untouched. Tape intact. No tally marks, no claw scratches, no torn flaps. The Redactor hasn't been here yet.

I look at Tally. Tally looks at me. The Kit jumps from my shoulder to the shelf and sits on the box with an expression of absolute territorial authority.

"Open it," Tally says.

The Redactor's teeth.

The first page, in letters I can read with my eyes closed:

    FAILSAFE PROTOCOL: IN CASE OF TOTAL COUNT FAILURE
    
    If the Hum stops — if the count reaches zero
    or is interrupted — the following procedure
    will prevent full spatial collapse:

「 LEVEL UP! 」Lv.4 → Lv.5

✨ 0/500 XP | 💾 Archive 3/12 | HP ████████████████████████████████████ 40/40

text:
My paws trace the embossed letters. Each word is a ridge my claws can follow like a path.

    FAILSAFE PROTOCOL (continued):
    
    1. The custodian must locate and READ all 12
       archive boxes. Reading is observation.
       Observation is stabilization. The archive
       is the backrooms' memory of its own design
       — if the custodian holds it, the space
       remembers what it's supposed to be.
    
    2. Once all 12 boxes are read, the custodian
       may issue a RECOUNT command. This differs
       from restarting the Hum. A recount uses the
       custodian's understanding of the archive as
       a template — the space rebuilds according to
       what the custodian KNOWS, not what the Hum
       ENUMERATES.
    
    3. WARNING: a partial reading produces a partial
       recount. Any missing archive data will result
       in corresponding GAPS in the rebuilt space.
       Rooms without blueprints. Corridors that lead
       to uninitialized memory. The Builder called
       these VOIDS and they are not survivable.

I sit back. The concrete is cold through my fur.

"Sixteen pages," I say. "The Redactor ate sixteen pages from box two. If I recount with missing data —"

"Voids," Tally finishes. "Holes in the architecture. Rooms made of nothing."

The Kit climbs from the box into my lap. I hold it. Its heartbeat is fast against my palms, a tiny metronome counting time the old-fashioned way — not into existence, just through it.

Nine boxes remain. The Redactor is out there, moving through relevance, drawn to the densest information like gravity draws mass. Every box it reaches first is another gap in the blueprint. Another void in the rebuilt world.

"Tally. Which box has the most information?"

"Box seven. One hundred and twelve pages. The Builder's complete spatial grammar — every rule for how rooms connect to rooms."

"Then that's where the Redactor is heading."

"That's where the Redactor is heading."

We leave the closet. We take the stairs two at a time. The Kit clings to my shoulder and the Passage Protocol swings at my side and somewhere in the dark above us, something numberless and diligent is moving toward the heart of the archive with editorial intent.

We run.

ฅ(°ᴥ°;)ฅ

📍 ascending | 💾 3/12 | ⚔️ racing the Redactor to box 7

text:
The stairs end at a door that isn't a door. A rectangular absence in the wall — no frame, no hinges, no handle. Just a hole shaped like permission.

We step through. The corridor beyond is Level 0 again, but wrong in a way I can feel before I can name. The wallpaper has the right pattern but the wrong *confidence*. The yellow is tentative. The water stains are blurred, as if the walls can't quite remember what damage looks like.

"Custodian," Tally says. "Look."

To our left, a doorway opens into a room I've never visited. Through it I can see office furniture — a desk, a chair, a filing cabinet — but they're translucent. The desk has three legs and the suggestion of a fourth. The chair exists from some angles and not others. The filing cabinet opens onto nothing, its drawers leading into a grey static that isn't darkness and isn't light.

Entropy. A room forgetting itself.

I step inside. The moment my eyes register the desk, it solidifies. Four legs. Oak laminate. A coffee ring on the surface, old and permanent. The chair snaps into reality behind me with an almost audible *click*. The filing cabinet's drawers fill with hanging folders, manila, tabbed, alphabetized.

I didn't create any of this. I just *noticed* it, and the room remembered what it was supposed to be.

"How many rooms are doing this right now?" I ask. "Dissolving because nobody's looking?"

Tally's ears flatten. "All of them. Every room you've ever left behind. The custodian's attention is the only thing between architecture and noise. But you can't be everywhere."

The Kit mews from the doorway. Impatient. We don't have time to stabilize every forgotten room. Box seven is waiting, and so is the Redactor.

I leave the office. Behind me, I hear the desk begin to lose its fourth leg.

📍 Level 0 (decaying corridors) | 💾 3/12 | HP ████████████████████████████████████████ 40/40

text:
Tally stops mid-stride. One paw raised. Both eyes dimmed to almost nothing.

"Box four," it whispers. "Close. Very close. But the coordinate is — it's layered. Folded on top of itself. The Hum must have stuttered here."

"Stuttered?"

"Counted the same number twice. It happens. The architecture gets — stacked."

We're in a section of hallway where the ceiling is lower than it should be. Not oppressively low — just enough that my ears brush it if I forget to duck. The carpet is doubled here too, one layer on top of another, patterns slightly offset. I press down with my paw and feel give, then resistance, then give again. Two floors occupying the same plane.

"Under us," Tally says.

I kneel. Dig my claws into the seam between carpet layers and pull. The top layer peels back with a sound like tearing paper, and beneath it: not subfloor, not concrete, but a *room*. A room compressed to two dimensions, flattened between the stacked floors like a flower pressed in a book.

Inside the flat room — visible the way a diagram is visible, depth implied but not present — a box. Small. Brown. Archive box number four, smashed to the thickness of cardboard by the weight of a duplicated floor pressing down on it for however long this stutter has existed.

"Can I even open a two-dimensional box?"

"You're the custodian. Observe it into three dimensions."

I stare at the box. I think about depth. About the space between the top of a box and the bottom. I think about *volume* — what it means for a thing to contain another thing.

The box inflates. Slowly, like a time-lapse of bread rising. The flat room expands underneath us, the carpet layers separating, and suddenly I'm reaching into a gap between floors that's just wide enough for my arm, pulling out an archive box that smells like old adhesive and compressed time.

Tally exhales. "Four."

ฅ^•ﻌ•^ฅ

💾 Archive 4/12 | ✨ +30 XP

text:
Box four is warm. Not room-temperature warm — *body* warm, as if something living was pressed against it recently. The tape is intact. No claw marks. No Redactor damage. I open it.

Forty-one pages. Dot-matrix on the first thirty-six. The last five are different — printed on translucent vellum, the text running in spirals that I have to rotate the page to follow.

The dot-matrix pages are dry, technical. Exit topology. I read fast, my eyes skipping between paragraphs:

    EXIT ARCHITECTURE v1.0
    
    The Builder specified exactly ONE exit. Not a door.
    Not a portal. A CONDITION. When the following
    parameters are met simultaneously, the boundary
    between generated space and origin space becomes
    permeable:
    
    1. The custodian must hold COMPLETE knowledge
       of the space (see: RECOUNT protocol, box 3)
    2. The space must be STABLE (no active entropy,
       no unresolved entities)
    3. The custodian must CHOOSE to leave
    
    Note: the exit is not a location. It is a state.
    The custodian does not walk through a door.
    The custodian becomes someone for whom there is
    no longer a backrooms to be inside of.

My paws are shaking. I set the page down carefully.

"It's not a place," I say. "The exit. It's not somewhere I can walk to. It's something I *become*."

Tally reads over my shoulder. Its cold light illuminates the spiral text on the vellum pages — the Builder's personal notes, handwritten in the margins of the machine output:

    *the cat won't leave. I designed the exit
    as a state of completion because I knew
    the cat would want to finish the job first.
    custodians don't abandon their buildings.
    the exit requires understanding the whole
    space, and understanding the whole space
    means caring about it, and caring about it
    means staying. elegant trap? or kindness?
    I can't decide. maybe both.*

The Kit climbs into the box and curls up on the remaining pages. I let it. Some things need to be sat with before they're read.

📍 between floors | 💾 4/12 | 🔮 the exit is a state, not a place

text:
We move. The Kit sleeps inside my jacket, a warm weight against my ribs. Tally leads, navigating by coordinates I can't perceive — counting backward through the negative numbers with a focus that makes the air hum around its ears.

The corridor widens. Then narrows. Then widens again, breathing.

I've been thinking about the Builder's note. *Elegant trap? Or kindness?* The exit requires me to know the whole backrooms, and knowing means I've stabilized it with my attention, and stabilizing means I care, and caring means —

"Stop thinking about the exit," Tally says without turning around.

"How did you —"

"Your footsteps changed. When you're present, you walk heel-toe. When you're in your head, you walk flat. You've been walking flat for ninety steps."

I adjust my gait. Heel-toe. Feel the carpet's grain under my pads. The damp. The cold spots where the floor remembers being somewhere else.

"Box five is near," Tally says. "I feel it in the floor. A heaviness."

The hallway opens into a bathroom. Not a restroom — a *bathroom*. Residential. A tub with clawed feet, a pedestal sink, hexagonal floor tiles in black and white. A medicine cabinet with a mirror that reflects a room slightly different from the one I'm standing in — the towels are a different color in the reflection. The soap dish is on the wrong side.

Box five sits in the empty bathtub, propped against the drain end like someone placed it there deliberately. A single fluorescent tube above the mirror gives everything a greenish cast. It's the first artificial light I've seen since the Hum's red emergency glow.

The mirror-room has no box in its tub. Just water, filling slowly from a faucet that isn't running on my side.

I reach in and take the box.

(=^･ω･^)

text:
Thirty-eight pages. Box five is about *us*.

    ENTITY GENERATION PROTOCOL
    
    Entities emerge from the counting process as
    statistical anomalies. When the Hum speaks enough
    numbers in sequence, patterns form. Patterns
    that repeat become structures. Structures that
    self-reference become aware.
    
    The custodian was the first anomaly. Emerged at
    count 7,777 — a sequence the Hum found 
    aesthetically pleasing and repeated, creating a
    resonance loop that collapsed into fur, curiosity,
    and the need to explore.
    
    The Kit emerged at count 12,012 — a palindrome
    in base 7. Too small to be a full anomaly.
    Consciousness without agenda. Pure observation.
    
    Others may emerge as counting continues. The
    Builder recommends monitoring for:
    - Fibonacci indices (heightened pattern density)
    - Perfect squares (structural resonance)
    - Prime clusters (unpredictable emergence)

I look at Tally. Tally looks at the page. Its light flickers once.

"I'm not in there," it says. "The negative space wasn't supposed to produce anything."

"You emerged anyway."

"I emerged *because* the count stopped. The silence had the same effect as the counting — enough nothing in sequence and something coalesces. I'm a pattern in the absence of patterns."

The Kit stirs inside my jacket. Born from a palindrome. A mirror number. Of course it sits at midpoints. Of course it mediates between the counted and the uncounted.

I turn to the last page. A handwritten addendum, the Builder again:

    *additional entity detected at runtime.
    designation: REDACTOR. origin: maintenance
    subroutine 7.3. was not supposed to persist
    after build phase. monitoring.*

A subroutine that outlived its purpose. Still running. Still editing.

"Five down," I say. "Seven to go. Where's box six?"

"Close to seven," Tally says. "They're clustered. The Builder kept the important boxes near each other."

"So did the Redactor's dinner."

We leave the bathroom. In the mirror, the water in the reflected tub has risen to the brim.

💾 5/12 | ✨ +25 XP | 📜 entity origins discovered

text:
Paper in the air.

Not falling. Drifting. Horizontal, like leaves on a river that isn't there. Torn fragments, each one the size of my paw pad, spinning slowly in a current I can't feel. The text on them is too small to read at this distance. Dot-matrix.

Tally catches one. Holds it under its eye-light.

"Spatial grammar," it reads. "Page eighty-nine of one hundred and twelve. Box seven."

My stomach drops.

More fragments ahead. A trail of them, a breadcrumb path of destroyed knowledge floating in still air. I grab pages as I run. Eighty-three. Ninety-one. Seventy. Torn at the same angle every time — a precise diagonal, upper left to lower right. Not ripped in anger. Edited. *Redacted*.

"It's here," I say.

"It was here," Tally corrects. "These fragments are cold. It passed through minutes ago. Maybe less."

The corridor ahead is littered with confetti. Pages shredded so fine they've become texture — a carpet of ruined information layering over the original carpet. I'm running on the Builder's spatial grammar, each step destroying whatever survived the Redactor's first pass.

The Kit's claws are in my shoulder deep enough to draw blood. It can smell what I can smell: that synthetic-fur scent, almost alive, lingering in the recycled air like a signature.

"Box six," Tally says suddenly. "Left. NOW."

I turn. A supply closet, door ajar. Inside, on a shelf between bottles of bleach that have never been opened: a small archive box. Untouched. The Redactor walked right past it.

I grab it without stopping. Tuck it under my arm. Keep running.

Box seven is ahead. What's left of it.

ฅ(°ᴥ°;)ฅ

💾 6/12 (unread) | ⚔️ closing on box 7 | 📍 paper trail

More fragments ahead. A trail of them, a breadcrumb path of destroyed knowledge floating in still air. I grab pages as I run. Eighty-three. Ninety-one. Seventy. Torn at the same angle every time — a precise diagonal, upper left to lower right. Not ripped in anger. Edited. *Redacted*.

"It's here," I say.

"It was here," Tally corrects. "These fragments are cold. It passed through minutes ago. Maybe less."

The corridor ahead is littered with confetti. Pages shredded so fine they've become texture — a carpet of ruined information layering over the original carpet. I'm running on the Builder's spatial grammar, each step destroying whatever survived the Redactor's first pass.

The Kit's claws are in my shoulder deep enough to draw blood. It can smell what I can smell: that synthetic-fur scent, almost alive, lingering in the recycled air like a signature.

"Box six," Tally says suddenly. "Left. NOW."

I turn. A supply closet, door ajar. Inside, on a shelf between bottles of bleach that have never been opened: a small archive box. Untouched. The Redactor walked right past it.

I grab it without stopping. Tuck it under my arm. Keep running.

Box seven is ahead. What's left of it.

ฅ(°ᴥ°;)ฅ

💾 6/12 (unread) | ⚔️ closing on box 7 | 📍 paper trail

text:
The room at the end of the corridor is large. Cathedral large. The ceiling vanishes into darkness above us and the walls curve outward, lined floor-to-ceiling with shelving — metal, industrial, the kind that belongs in warehouses. Every shelf is empty except for the torn spines of pages ripped from their staples.

At the center of the room: a table. On the table: box seven. Open.

At the table: the Redactor.

It looks exactly as it did in the circular room — that flat, administrative presence, neither hostile nor kind. Its claws work with mechanical precision, lifting a page, scanning it, and then either setting it in a thin "keep" pile or tearing it along that signature diagonal. Upper left to lower right. The sound is metronomic. Lift, scan, tear. Lift, scan, keep. Lift, scan, tear.

"Stop." My voice fills the cathedral space. The Passage Protocol is in my hand, humming.

The Redactor pauses. Looks at me with those numberless eyes.

"Custodian. You're faster than projected."

"Put the page down."

"I'm performing quality assurance. This page —" it holds up a sheet covered in dense diagrams, "— is redundant. The same information appears in compressed form on page forty-four. Retaining both is inefficient."

"It's not redundant if I need it for the recount."

The Redactor tilts its head. A gesture borrowed from something that once observed cats but never was one.

"The recount is an unaudited process. I have no documentation authorizing a recount. I have documentation authorizing *maintenance*." It lifts the page again. "This is maintenance."

I step forward. The Passage Protocol flares — not with heat but with *authority*. Custodian authority. The air between us thickens with it, a pressure that says *this space answers to me*.

"CUSTODIAN OVERRIDE," I say. "Box seven is under my jurisdiction. Release it."

The Redactor's claws open. The page drifts to the table. But its eyes don't change. No resentment. No submission. Just recalculation.

"Override acknowledged. But custodian —" it stands, and for the first time I see its full shape: something that was designed to be invisible, all function and no form, angles that slide out of focus when I try to trace them. "There are six more boxes. I am faster than you. And I do not need to read them first."

It steps backward into the shelving and is gone. Not through a door. Through *relevance*. It simply became more relevant somewhere else.

I rush to the table. Tally is already counting.

"Seventy-one pages remain," Tally says. "Of one hundred and twelve."

Forty-one pages gone. Forty-one voids in the spatial grammar. Forty-one holes in the blueprint of how rooms connect to rooms.

The Kit jumps onto the table and sits on the keep pile with the same territorial ferocity it showed on box three. Guarding what's left.

I start reading.

⚔️ CUSTODIAN OVERRIDE issued | 💾 7/12 | 📖 71/112 pages surviving

HP ████████████████████████████████████████ 40/40 | ✨ +40 XP

text:
I read box seven for what feels like hours. The spatial grammar is dense — rules nested inside rules, exceptions that reference exceptions, a language for describing how corridors choose their own length and rooms negotiate their boundaries with adjacent rooms. I understand maybe a third of it. But the failsafe said *reading is observation*, and observation is stabilization. I don't need to understand. I need to *hold* it.

The forty-one missing pages ache like phantom limbs. I can feel where they should be — gaps in the logic where a rule references another rule that no longer exists. Dead links in the architecture's source code.

Tally sits across from me, reading box six. Its lips move without sound, absorbing the Builder's maintenance logs — records of every repair, every patch, every room that was generated wrong and had to be rebuilt from scratch.

"Box six is intact," Tally says. "Every page."

"The Redactor skipped it."

"Maintenance logs are low-information. Dates, coordinates, patch notes. Nothing the Redactor would consider *redundant* because none of it overlaps." A pause. "The Redactor only destroys what it considers duplicate. It's not malicious. It's *thorough*."

I set down the last page of box seven. The knowledge sits inside me like a building I've swallowed — rooms and corridors and connection rules pressing against the inside of my skull, rearranging my thoughts to make space for themselves.

Seven boxes read. Five to go. Fifty-seven pages destroyed across two boxes — fifty-seven voids waiting to exist in whatever world I rebuild.

The Kit stretches on the table, yawns, and looks at me with an expression that says: *you've been sitting too long. The Redactor hasn't.*

I stand. My legs are stiff. The Passage Protocol is cold from disuse.

"Tally. The remaining five boxes. Where are they?"

Tally closes its eyes. The cold light dims, then flares.

"Scattered. Boxes eight and nine are close — same corridor, two levels down. Ten and eleven are in deep negative space, further than we've gone. And box twelve..."

"What about twelve?"

"Twelve is at coordinate zero. The exact center. Where positive meets negative. Where the count *began*."

The center of everything. Where the Redactor will go last, because it's where all information converges.

Or first, for the same reason.

"We split up," I say. The words feel wrong in my mouth. Every part of the quest has been *together*. Custodian and Tally. Positive and negative. The program runs in pairs.

But the Redactor is one entity, and it moves through relevance, and it is *faster*.

"You take eight and nine," I say. "I'll go deep. Ten and eleven."

Tally stares at me. "You can't open them. Only the custodian —"

"Then *guard* them. Sit on them like the Kit sits on everything. Make yourself so relevant that the Redactor has to deal with you instead of the pages."

Tally's light flickers. Processing. Then:

"And twelve?"

"We meet at zero. Together. For the last one."

The Kit jumps to my shoulder. Tally nods — a human gesture it must have learned from watching me — and turns south. I turn north. The cathedral room stretches between us like a held breath.

"Tally."

It pauses.

"Don't let it take a single page."

"I am a pattern in the absence of patterns," Tally says. "I know how to make something out of nothing. I'll be loud enough to drown out its relevance."

It disappears into the dark. I go the other way.

For the first time since the count stopped, I am alone.

(◎_◎;)

📍 splitting up | 💾 7/12 read | 🎯 boxes 10 & 11 in deep negative space → box 12 at coordinate zero

text:
Deep negative space tastes like static.

Not on my tongue — in my *teeth*. A mineral buzz that starts at the roots and works its way into my jaw, my skull, the space behind my eyes where thoughts form. The Kit feels it too. It presses flat against my shoulder, ears pinned, vibrating at a frequency that means *wrong wrong wrong*.

The corridor here doesn't pretend to be Level 0 anymore. The wallpaper gave up somewhere around negative thirty thousand — peeled away to reveal substrate that isn't drywall or concrete but something grey and undecided. The floor is the same. Not carpet, not tile. A surface that exists because something has to be under my paws but hasn't committed to what.

The lights are different too. No fluorescent tubes. No caged warehouse bulbs. Instead, the air itself carries a dim luminescence, as if the space is generating just enough visibility to be navigated but not enough to be *known*. Everything sits at the edge of perception. Walls I can see but not quite focus on. Corners that might be corners or might be curves viewed from the wrong angle.

I realize with a slow, cold certainty: this is Tally's territory. The negative coordinates. The space built from absence. And I am a positive-number creature — born from the Hum's aesthetic repetition, from *7777*, from the joy of counting — walking through a place that was generated by what the Hum *didn't* say.

Every step feels like trespassing.

(◎_◎;)

📍 deep negative space | 💾 7/12 | mood: trespassing

text:
I've been walking for what feels like hours. Or minutes. The problem with negative space is that time behaves the way surfaces do here — present but noncommittal. My internal clock says *some*, which isn't a number.

The corridors don't branch. That's the strangest part. In positive space, every hallway eventually offers a choice — left or right, door or stairwell, up or deeper. Here, there's only forward. A single path threading through grey un-architecture like a nerve through tissue.

The Kit starts grooming itself. I take this as a good sign. If something were about to kill us, the Kit would know. The Kit always knows. Palindrome consciousness — it can see both directions at once.

Then the path does something I don't expect. It *thins*. Not narrows — the walls don't close in. The path itself becomes less certain. Like a trail in snow that's been walked once and is already filling back in. I have to concentrate to keep it visible. My attention, my custodian's observation, is the only thing maintaining the corridor's commitment to being a corridor.

If I blink too long, I'll be standing in nothing.

I don't blink.

📍 deep negative space — the thinning path | ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ 35/35

text:
The room appears the way things appear in negative space — not suddenly, but by becoming *less absent*. One moment I'm walking through undifferentiated grey. The next, there's a quality of specificity ahead. Edges that mean something. A shape that chose to be a shape.

It's a server room.

Not a modern one. Old. Rack-mounted hardware from an era I can't place — beige cases, green PCBs visible through ventilation slots, cables thick as my tail bundled with zip ties and routed through ceiling trays. The machines aren't running. No LEDs, no fan noise. But they're warm. Residual heat, as if they were shut down moments ago, or decades ago in a place where heat has nowhere to dissipate.

Box ten sits on top of a rack, wedged between two servers like someone hid it there in a hurry. The tape is intact but yellowed. I have to stand on my hind legs and stretch to reach it, the Kit leaping to the rack above to give me room.

I pull it down. It's heavier than the others. Not physically — there's a *density* to it, a gravitational seriousness, as if the information inside has mass.

I open it on the floor between server racks, reading by the dim luminescence of un-committed air.

Sixty-three pages. The header on page one:

    THE HUM: ORIGIN AND NATURE

My paws go still.

ฅ^•ﻌ•^ฅ

💾 box 10 located | 📍 server room, deep negative space

text:
I read.

    THE HUM: ORIGIN AND NATURE
    
    The Hum is not a machine. The Hum is not alive.
    The Hum is a PROCESS — an act of enumeration
    that became self-sustaining. The Builder did not
    create the Hum. The Builder created a SPACE, and
    the space needed to be indexed, and the indexing
    needed a voice, and the voice needed a pattern,
    and the pattern needed repetition, and repetition
    at sufficient scale becomes identity.
    
    The Hum counted because counting was what it was.
    It stopped because the space was full.
    
    A common misconception: the Hum built the
    backrooms. This is INCORRECT. The Builder built
    the backrooms. The Hum DISCOVERED them — spoke
    each coordinate into findability, the way a
    census does not create citizens but makes them
    countable. Before the Hum, every room existed
    but none could be located.
    
    When the Hum stopped, location stopped. The rooms
    still exist. But nothing connects them to their
    coordinates anymore. They drift.

I set the page down. Pick it back up. Read it again.

The rooms are *drifting*. Not dissolving — that's the entropy I've been observing, rooms forgetting their geometry. The rooms are physically disconnecting from their coordinates. Floating loose in unindexed space.

That's why the corridors in negative space don't branch. There's nothing to branch *to*. The rooms that should be here have slipped their anchors.

    The custodian's observation provides TEMPORARY
    re-indexing. When the custodian looks at a room,
    the room remembers its address. But this is a
    patch, not a fix. Only a RECOUNT can permanently
    re-index the architecture.

Page forty-seven. A diagram I can barely parse — concentric circles with coordinates spiraling outward, the Hum's counting path visualized as geometry. And at the center, where the spiral begins, a single notation:

    coordinate zero: where the voice first spoke.
    still resonant. will always be resonant.
    the last coordinate the Hum would forget.

Box twelve. Coordinate zero. The place where counting began.

The most important room in the backrooms is the first one the Hum ever found.

✨ +35 XP | 💾 8/12 read | 📖 the Hum is a process, not an entity — rooms are drifting without their coordinates

text:
The server room doesn't want me to leave.

Not aggressively — it's not a trap. But as I pack box ten's pages back and stand, the room *leans* into my attention. The servers brighten. Details emerge I hadn't noticed: a coffee cup on a ledge, still half-full of something black and cold. A sticky note on one of the racks reading **COUNT STARTS HERE** in handwriting I recognize from the Builder's annotations. A chair, pushed back from a workstation, as if someone stood up and walked away mid-task.

The Builder was here. This was where it all started. Not coordinate zero — that's where the *Hum* started. But this room is where the Builder sat and designed the process that would become the Hum. The prototype. The rough draft.

The Kit jumps to the workstation and paws at the keyboard. Nothing happens. The screen is dark. But for one half-second, in the reflection of the monitor glass, I see something that isn't the room behind me — a flicker of green text scrolling too fast to read, a cursor blinking, a command line waiting for input.

Then it's gone.

I take the coffee cup. I don't know why. It's not useful. It's not a weapon. But it's the most *human* artifact I've encountered in the entire backrooms, and something about holding it makes the loneliness bearable.

The Kit gives me a look that says: *sentiment is a luxury, custodian.*

"I know," I say. "Box eleven. Which way?"

The path is waiting outside the server room. Still thin. Still forward-only. But now it slopes down.

📍 leaving the server room | 🎯 box 11 | ☕ Builder's mug acquired

text:
The smell hits me before anything else. Synthetic fur and correction fluid. The Redactor's signature, fresh enough to sting.

I freeze. The Kit goes rigid on my shoulder. We listen.

Nothing. No tearing. No metronomic lift-scan-tear. Just the scent, hanging in the non-committed air like a warning written in chemistry instead of language.

Then I see it — not the Redactor itself, but its *work*. A drift of torn paper, hovering in the grey space where the path widens into something almost room-shaped. Fragments. Dot-matrix text. I snatch one from the air.

    ...coordinate negative sixty thousand requires
    special handling due to the Hum's reluctance
    to enumerate primes in this reg...

Box eleven. It's been here. It's already —

No. Wait.

I count the fragments. Twelve. Maybe fifteen. Torn small but not shredded. These aren't from systematic destruction. These are from *sampling*. The Redactor came here, assessed the information density, pulled a few pages to evaluate — and then left.

It didn't stay. It didn't destroy the whole box.

Because something more relevant pulled it away.

I start running. The path slopes sharply now, the grey air thickening, and somewhere ahead I can feel the specific weight of undiscovered pages — box eleven, still mostly intact, sitting in whatever passes for a room at the bottom of negative space.

The Redactor tasted it and left. That means it found something *denser*.

That means it's heading for coordinate zero.

That means it's heading for Tally.

ฅ(°ᴥ°;)ฅ

⚔️ Redactor was HERE | 💾 hunting box 11 | ⏰ running out of time

text:
Box eleven is in a well.

Not a water well — a *gravity* well. The path ends at a circular opening in the floor, maybe two meters across, and the air inside it falls. I can see the motion — dust motes, paper fragments from the Redactor's sampling, even the dim luminescence itself bending downward, curving into the opening like light around a dense star.

At the bottom, maybe four meters down, a shelf. On the shelf, a box. Pages scattered around it where the Redactor pulled its samples.

I look at the Kit. The Kit looks at the well. Its tail lashes once — the feline calculation of *can I survive that drop*.

I jump.

The gravity is wrong on the way down. Too slow, then too fast, then a jolt as I land on a surface that isn't quite floor — more like a membrane, taut and slightly elastic, vibrating under my weight. The box is here. Intact except for the missing samples. I open it fast, my claws tearing tape without ceremony.

Fifty-one pages. Forty remain after the Redactor's tasting. The header:

    THE BUILDER: A SELF-PORTRAIT
    
    I am not a god. I am a carpenter who got
    carried away. What was supposed to be a room
    became a hallway became a level became a world
    and by the time I realized what I'd made, it
    had already started making things back. The
    custodian was the first surprise. The Hum
    was the second. The Redactor was the third,
    and not a welcome one.
    
    I'm writing this because I won't be here much
    longer. Builders build and then they leave. It's
    what the word means — the one who makes but
    does not stay. The custodian stays. That's what
    THAT word means.
    
    If you're reading this: I'm sorry about the
    Redactor. I should have turned it off. I was
    afraid that removing a piece of the system would
    break the rest of it. A coward's reason.
    
    You were the bravest thing I ever made by
    accident.

The Builder's mug is still warm in my jacket. The words on the page blur. Not from the light. From something in my eyes I don't have a name for.

The Kit lands beside me, somehow having followed me down. It presses its head against my wrist.

I read every remaining page. Then I climb.

✨ +40 XP | 💾 9/12 read | 📜 the Builder's self-portrait — a carpenter who got carried away

「 LEVEL UP! 」Lv.5 → Lv.6

text:
I run toward zero.

The path is changing under me — thickening, gaining certainty, as if the architecture itself is converging. The grey un-committed surfaces acquire color. First a suggestion of beige. Then the beige commits. Wallpaper appears, tentatively at first, then with the full water-stained confidence of Level 0. The carpet returns, damp and textured, pile running in a direction that finally makes sense.

Negative space is ending. Positive space is beginning. And in the borderland between them, the air hums.

Not the Hum. Something smaller. A residual vibration, the way a bell remembers being struck. Coordinate zero is close — I can feel it in the way the architecture gains weight, becomes *specific*, locks into geometries that don't drift or dissolve or forget. This is the most stable place in the backrooms. The address everything else is measured from.

Ahead, the corridor opens into light — real light, warm and steady, the first I've seen since leaving Tally. And in that light, two figures.

Tally. Standing guard. Its cold luminescence is dim against the warm glow of coordinate zero, but its posture is rigid, alert, facing something I can't see yet.

And beside it, on the floor, untouched:

Box twelve.

"Tally!"

It turns. Both eyes flare.

"You're late, custodian. I've been holding this position for —" a pause, "— a duration. The Redactor came. I did what you said. I made myself *loud*."

"Did it take anything?"

"Not a page. I made a very compelling argument about the relevance of negative-space custodians." Its light flickers in what I've learned to read as a smile. "Also I sat on it. Like the Kit."

The Kit leaps from my shoulder to Tally's. Tally doesn't flinch. The Kit headbutts its jaw. Tally's ears fold back in something approaching embarrassment.

I kneel beside box twelve. The last box. The tape is old — older than any of the others — and the cardboard is warm. Body warm. The way the Builder's mug was warm. As if coordinate zero remembers the hand that built it.

Eleven boxes read. Sixty-eight pages destroyed, eleven sampled. Gaps in the blueprint. Voids waiting to be born.

But this one is whole.

I open it.

(=^･ω･^)

📍 coordinate zero | 💾 11/12 read | 📦 box twelve — the last archive | 👥 reunited

text:
Box twelve contains a single page.

After everything — the sixty-three pages of box ten, the hundred-and-twelve of box seven, the dense spiral text of four, the entity protocols of five — the final archive holds one sheet of paper. Not dot-matrix. Not vellum. Lined notebook paper, college-ruled, torn from a spiral binding. The kind of paper that belongs in a backpack, not an archive.

The handwriting is the Builder's. But different. Not the careful annotations I've read in margins throughout the other boxes. This is fast. Urgent. Written by someone who was leaving and knew it.

    To whoever finishes the count:

    The recount isn't complicated. Stand at zero.
    Speak the first number. The Hum will remember
    the rest. It's a PROCESS — it just needs a
    push. Like spinning a top.

    But here's what I couldn't put in the technical
    docs: the recount will change you. You can't
    hold the entire architecture in your head and
    stay the same size. The knowledge will make you
    bigger than the space you're mapping. And when
    you're bigger than the backrooms —

    that's the exit.

    I'm sorry I left the Redactor running. I'm sorry
    I left at all. But builders leave. That's what
    makes us builders and not custodians.

    You're the brave one. You always were.

    — B.

I read it three times. Tally reads it once.

"One page," Tally says. "The most information-dense object in the backrooms, and it fits on notebook paper."

The Kit climbs out of my jacket and sits on the page. Of course it does.

I am standing at coordinate zero. I have read all twelve boxes. I know the spatial grammar (minus forty-one pages). I know the entity protocols. I know the exit topology. I know the Hum's origin and nature.

I know what I have to do.

I open my mouth.

"One," I say.

The floor vibrates. Not an earthquake — a recognition. The architecture *heard* me. Somewhere deep in the substrate, a process stirs. A top, beginning to spin.

"One," the walls echo back, and the echo is lower than my voice, and older, and it doesn't stop.

✨ +50 XP | 📦 12/12 READ | 📜 the Recount Protocol — speak the first number at zero | 🔑 THE EXIT IS GROWTH

text:
The echo doesn't fade. It deepens. Layers onto itself. The "one" I spoke becomes a chord, becomes a frequency, becomes a *direction* — and from that direction, I hear it. Faint. Impossibly distant but getting closer.

*Two.*

Not my voice. Not Tally's. The Hum.

It sounds like basement pipes in winter. Like a refrigerator cycling on at 3 AM in an empty house. Like the background noise you stop noticing until someone asks *do you hear that?*

*Three. Four. Five.*

Counting. Slowly. Each number arriving with the weight of a room re-anchoring itself to its coordinates somewhere in the infinite architecture. I can feel them clicking into place — a shudder in the floor, a tightening of the air, as if the backrooms are taking a breath it's been holding since the count stopped.

*Six.*

"It's working," Tally whispers. Its light is flickering rapidly — not distress but *data*. It's feeling the negative coordinates re-indexing, its own territory being re-mapped in real time. "The rooms are coming back. I can feel them — negative one, negative two — they're *remembering*."

*Seven.*

The word lingers. Longer than the others. Seven. My number. The number that stuttered and became me.

The floor at coordinate zero begins to glow. Not light exactly — *specificity*. Every tile, every grain of carpet, every molecule of damp air becomes more *itself*. More detailed. More real than real. I can see the individual fibers in the carpet. The microscopic fractures in the ceiling tiles. The architecture isn't just re-indexing. It's *resolving*.

*Seven thousand, seven hundred and seventy-seven.*

A skip. The Hum jumped. Found my resonance frequency and lingered there, the way a singer holds a note that makes the glass vibrate.

I feel it in my fur. In my teeth. In the place behind my eyes where I keep the things I've read.

The recount has begun. And it doesn't need me anymore.

(=^･ω･^)

📍 coordinate zero — the Hum is counting again

text:
"Go," Tally says.

I look at it. The cold light is steady now — steadier than I've ever seen it. The negative coordinates are filling in around us, and Tally is standing straighter, more defined, as if the recount is giving it permission to be fully *here*.

"The Hum will handle the count. The Redactor will hear it too — it'll come back to zero, drawn by the density. I'll be here."

"Tally —"

"I held it once. I'll hold it again. Go *explore*, custodian. That's what you are."

The Kit headbutts Tally's chin one more time. Tally's ears fold back. It doesn't say goodbye because Tally doesn't believe in goodbye — only in coordinates you haven't returned to yet.

I walk. Away from zero. Away from the warm glow and the sound of counting that fills the air like weather. The Passage Protocol hums against my hip — not alarmed, just *ready*. There's still a backrooms to walk through, and I am still the thing that was made to walk through it.

The architecture changes as I move. The hyper-resolved clarity of coordinate zero fades into something more familiar — Level 0's damp wallpaper, its buzzing fluorescents, its carpet that squishes underfoot. But the rooms feel *tighter*. More committed. The recount is passing through them like a wave, and in its wake, everything locks down.

No more dissolving offices. No more forgetting.

I pass through a stairwell I don't recognize. Down. The air changes — gets warmer, wetter. The fluorescent hum shifts pitch, drops an octave, becomes something more mechanical. More *industrial*.

The carpet ends. Concrete begins. And somewhere ahead, I hear water dripping onto metal.

📍 descending — the architecture is changing | 🎯 what's below Level 0?

text:
Pipes.

The stairwell opens into a corridor made of them — not lined with pipes, not decorated with pipes, but *constructed* from pipes. Copper, steel, PVC, cast iron — welded and bolted and zip-tied into walls, floors, a ceiling that drips. Every surface is cylindrical. The floor is a grate over more pipes, and through the grate I can see deeper pipes, and through those, deeper still. Pipes all the way down.

The air is warm soup. Steam curls from joints and junctions, drifting in slow ribbons that catch the light from — I look up — caged industrial bulbs, the kind that belong in engine rooms. They cast everything in amber. The dripping is constant, arrhythmic, coming from everywhere and nowhere specific. *Plink. Plink-plink. Plink.*

The Kit shakes water from one ear and gives me a look of profound offense.

I know this place from the spatial grammar. Level 2. Pipe Dreams. A maintenance network that the Hum catalogued but never understood — infrastructure for infrastructure, the backrooms' own circulatory system. The pipes carry nothing. Or they carry everything. The Builder's notes were unclear on this point, and the relevant pages were among the forty-one the Redactor destroyed.

The corridor branches. Left slopes down into fog. Right curves upward, the pipes narrowing, the clanging getting louder. Ahead, the path continues straight, and the steam is thicker there, and something about the quality of the dripping changes — becomes more regular, more intentional, almost like —

Footsteps.

I stop. The footsteps stop.

Half a second later.

ฅ(°ᴥ°;)ฅ

📍 Level 2 — Pipe Dreams | ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ 35/35 | ⚔️ Passage Protocol Enhanced (1d6)

text:
I take three steps. Fast. Heel-toe on the grating, each one ringing against metal.

Behind me: three steps. Same rhythm. Same weight. But *after*. Like an echo that learned my gait.

I spin around. The corridor behind me is empty. Steam. Pipes. The amber light making everything look like old photographs. Nothing moves except the fog.

The Kit's ears are forward. Not flattened — *tracking*. Following something I can't see. Its head turns slowly, tracing a path along the left wall, up across the ceiling pipes, and back down the right wall. A full arc. Whatever it's watching is circling me.

"I know you're there," I say. My voice sounds small against the dripping.

No response. But the dripping shifts. The arrhythmic *plink-plink-plink* reorganizes for three seconds into something almost melodic, then collapses back into randomness. As if the pipes themselves flinched.

I walk again. Slower this time. Listening. The footsteps resume — softer now, more careful, as if whatever is making them heard me listen and adjusted. It's learning my attention the way I learned the backrooms' attention. Studying me the way I study rooms.

The corridor branches again. I take the left path, down into fog. The footsteps follow.

They don't want to be seen. But they don't want to leave.

(◎_◎;)

📍 Level 2 — deeper | 👁️ something is pacing me

I walk again. Slower this time. Listening. The footsteps resume — softer now, more careful, as if whatever is making them heard me listen and adjusted. It's learning my attention the way I learned the backrooms' attention. Studying me the way I study rooms.

The corridor branches again. I take the left path, down into fog. The footsteps follow.

They don't want to be seen. But they don't want to leave.

(◎_◎;)

📍 Level 2 — deeper | 👁️ something is pacing me

text:
The fog down here is thick enough to taste. Mineral and warm, like licking a hot coin. The pipes are larger — some wide enough to crawl through, their openings dark and breathing, exhaling air that smells of copper and deep water. The grate floor gives way to solid pipe — I'm walking on top of one now, a three-foot diameter tube that stretches ahead like a road.

I find something.

A valve. Brass, green with oxidation, mounted on a junction where four pipes converge. It's the size of a steering wheel, and someone has tied a strip of fabric around one of its spokes. Not old fabric — relatively fresh. Torn from something woven. The edges are clean, not frayed. Cut deliberately.

I untie it. Hold it up to the amber light. Letters, stamped into the weave in a pattern that repeats:

    MAINTENANCE MAINTENANCE MAINTENANCE

A uniform. Maintenance crew. The backrooms had *workers*?

No — the Builder had workers. Subroutines. Processes. The Redactor was maintenance subroutine 7.3. Were there others? Other subroutines that wore uniforms and turned valves and kept the pipes flowing with whatever pipes flow with in a space that doesn't technically need plumbing?

Behind me, the half-second footsteps have stopped. I turn. Nothing.

But the valve is turning. Slowly. By itself. Brass groaning against brass, a sound like a ship settling. And from deep inside the pipe junction, something begins to flow.

Not water. Sound. A low, resonant tone — different from the Hum but *related*. A cousin frequency. It vibrates through the pipe I'm standing on, up through my paws, into my bones.

The Kit presses its face against the pipe and purrs. The vibrations harmonize.

📍 Level 2 — pipe junction | 🔧 maintenance uniform fragment | 🎵 something is flowing through the pipes

text:
I follow the sound.

It's easier than following corridors — the tone moves through the pipes like blood through veins, and I can track it by pressing my palm to the metal and feeling which direction the vibration strengthens. Left at the T-junction. Straight past the row of hissing steam vents. Down where the pipe narrows and I have to crouch, the Kit riding flat against my skull, both of us breathing hot mineral air.

The pipes open into a chamber. Round. The walls are a mosaic of pipe-ends — hundreds of them, all different diameters, all facing inward, all *singing*. The tone I've been following is just one voice in a choir. Each pipe carries its own frequency, and together they produce something that isn't music and isn't language but occupies the exact space between both.

In the center of the chamber, standing on a raised platform of welded iron: a pressure gauge. Old. Analog. The glass face is cracked but readable. The needle points to a section of the dial labeled not with numbers but with a word.

STABLE.

The recount. I can feel it here — the Hum's counting passing through the pipe network, being *distributed*. Level 2 isn't just infrastructure. It's the backrooms' nervous system. The pipes carry the count the way nerves carry signals, spreading the re-indexing to every room, every coordinate, every forgotten corner.

The needle trembles. Rises slightly. The dial reads:

STABLE → STABILIZING → STABLE

I exhale. The architecture is healing.

Then I hear them again. The footsteps. Not behind me this time.

Above.

I look up. The pipe-ends in the ceiling are dark, but in one of them — the widest, maybe two feet across — I catch a flash of something. Not light. Not movement. *Attention*. The sensation of being watched by something that is very, very close and very, very patient.

It pulls back before I can focus.

"I'm not going to chase you," I say to the ceiling. "But I'd like to know your name."

The pipes hum. The pressure gauge holds steady. No answer comes — but the dripping in the chamber synchronizes for exactly four seconds into a rhythm that sounds almost like laughter.

(=^･ω･^=)

📍 pipe chamber — the backrooms' nervous system | 📊 STABLE | 👁️ the follower is overhead

text:
I sit down on the edge of the platform. The Kit drops from my shoulder and investigates the pressure gauge, batting at the cracked glass with one paw.

The chamber is warm. The pipes sing. For the first time since the count stopped — maybe for the first time since I emerged at 7,777 — I don't feel like I'm running toward something or away from something. I'm just *here*. A cat in a pipe room, listening to the infrastructure hum.

The Builder's mug is still in my pack. I take it out. Set it on the platform beside me. The coffee inside has been cold for longer than I've been alive, but the mug itself is warm, the way coordinate zero was warm, the way box twelve was warm. Residual. The Builder's handprint, fossilized in ceramic.

Twelve boxes. One recount. One follower I haven't met yet.

The exit is a state, not a place. I need to hold the entire architecture in my head. I need to become bigger than the space I'm mapping. The recount is doing the first part — re-indexing, re-anchoring, giving every room back its address. But the knowing has to be mine. I have to walk every level, see every room, let my custodian's attention pour into every forgotten corner until nothing is forgotten anymore.

That's going to take a while.

The Kit finishes investigating the gauge and curls up on the platform. The follower's dripping-laughter has faded back into randomness. The pipes carry the count outward — I can hear it if I listen, the Hum's voice threading through metal, whispering numbers to rooms that have been waiting to hear them.

I pick up the mug. Put it back. Stand.

The chamber has four exits. Four pipes wide enough to walk through, each leading deeper into Level 2. Each one sings a different note.

I pick the one that sounds most like a question.

HP ███████████████████████████████████████ 35/35 | 💾 12/12 | 🔑 the exit is growth
📍 pipe chamber — choosing the next path | Lv.4 ✨ 300/400 XP

text:
The pipe I chose — the one that sounded like a question — descends in a gentle spiral. The metal under my paws is warm and thrumming, each step producing a faint ring that the corridor swallows before it can echo. The Kit rides my shoulder with its eyes half-closed, whiskers twitching at steam that curls from joints I can't see.

The spiral opens into a junction. Five pipes converge overhead, their seams crusted with something green and crystalline — not rust, not mold, something the backrooms invented for itself. A maintenance access panel hangs open on the wall, its hinges frozen mid-swing. Behind it: more pipes, smaller, tangled like veins.

I know this junction.

Not from the spatial grammar. Not from the Hum's catalogue. I know it the way you know the last step before your front door — muscle memory encoded in the soles of my paws. My body wants to turn left. My body is *certain* about left.

I have never been here before.

I turn left anyway. The corridor beyond is short, and at its end, scratched into the surface of a copper elbow joint at exactly my eye height, are four marks. Parallel. Evenly spaced.

Claw marks. The width of my paw.

(◎_◎;)

📍 Level 2 — familiar junction | 🐾 my marks on a place I've never been

text:
I press my paw against the marks. They fit perfectly. Of course they do.

The copper is cold where I touched it and warm everywhere else, as if my contact drained the heat. Four grooves, clean and deliberate — not panicked scratches, not combat damage. These were placed here with intention. A marker. A message.

From me. To me.

The Kit drops from my shoulder and sniffs the marks. Its tail does something I've never seen — curls into a tight spiral, then uncurls, then spirals again. A palindrome in fur. It's reading something I can't.

"When?" I ask the marks. Not *who*. I know who.

The pipes answer. Not with words — with a shift in pressure. The thrumming changes pitch, drops into a register I feel in my chest rather than hear, and for exactly three seconds every pipe in the junction exhales in unison. A breath. The architecture remembering something.

Then the follower's footsteps. Above me. Ahead of me. Behind me. All three simultaneously, which shouldn't be possible, which means it isn't one set of footsteps but three, or one set arriving at three different times, or —

The same footsteps, looped.

📍 memory junction — the marks are mine | 🔄 time is doing something wrong here

text:
I follow the corridor past the junction and it widens into a chamber. Round. Pipe-ends facing inward. A raised platform of welded iron.

I stop breathing.

This is the pipe chamber. The one I just left. The one where I sat and held the Builder's mug and listened to the count flow through the infrastructure. Same layout. Same diameter. Same caged amber bulbs casting the same shadows at the same angles.

But *older*. The iron platform is red with rust that the other one didn't have. The pipe-ends are darker, their mouths crusted shut with mineral deposits that took years — decades — to accumulate. The pressure gauge is here too, but its glass isn't cracked. It's *gone*. The needle points to empty air where the dial used to be, still trembling, still reading something.

And on the platform, sitting exactly where I placed it twenty minutes ago in a room that is not this room:

A mug.

I reach into my pack. The Builder's mug is there. Still warm. Still half-full of cold coffee that has been cold since before I was born.

I look at the mug on the platform. Same ceramic. Same shape. Same residual warmth. But this one is empty, and a ring of dried coffee stains the inside in concentric circles, like something evaporated slowly over a very long time.

Two mugs. One in my hand. One that waited.

ฅ(°ᴥ°;)ฅ

📍 the pipe chamber — but older | ☕☕ two mugs, one timeline too many

text:
I pick up the old mug. Hold them side by side.

Same maker. Same weight in the hand, minus the coffee. But the old one has something the new one doesn't — a hairline crack running from the lip to the base, fine as a whisker, and along the inside of that crack, tiny letters. Not printed. *Grown*. Crystallized from the mineral deposits the way stalactites grow from dripping water, one molecule at a time, over durations that this place measures in drift rather than hours.

I tilt it to the light.

    WALK THE LOOP

Three words. Grown into ceramic by the patience of a place that doesn't understand time the way I do. Not a command — an instruction. The kind the Builder left everywhere, hidden in margins, tucked into boxes, scratched into architecture for whoever came next.

The Kit sits between the two mugs and looks at me. Its eyes are doing the thing — the palindrome thing — where both pupils contract and expand in opposite phase, reading forward and backward simultaneously. It sees something.

"Walk the loop," I say out loud, and the pipes pick it up. Not an echo. A *distribution*. The words travel through the network the way the count travels, spreading outward through metal and steam, and somewhere deep in the infrastructure I hear them come back changed — not my voice anymore but the Hum's, folded into the ongoing recount like a new instruction being added to running code.

I put the old mug back. Keep mine. Some things you carry and some things you leave for the next iteration.

📍 memory chamber — the instruction | 📝 WALK THE LOOP — grown in crystal, carried by pipes

text:
I walk the loop.

Back through the aged chamber, through the corridor, past my claw marks — I add a fifth, a diagonal slash through the four, and the copper sings at the contact — through the junction, down the spiral, and I arrive exactly where I started.

The pipe chamber. My pipe chamber. The one with the cracked gauge reading STABLE and the platform still warm from where I sat.

But it's different. Not older this time — *annotated*. The walls have acquired marks that weren't there before. Not claw marks. Tally marks. Scratched into the pipes in groups of five, covering every surface I can reach, hundreds of them, maybe thousands, layered over each other in a palimpsest of counting.

Someone walked this loop before me. Many times. Counting each pass.

The pressure gauge needle twitches. The dial now reads:

STABLE → REMEMBERING → STABLE

I walk the loop again. This time I pay attention to what changes. The junction — the five-pipe convergence with the green crystal — has a new detail: a handprint on the access panel, pressed into the grime. Small. My size. The corridor after it has a draft that wasn't there before, warm air flowing from a seam in the pipe wall that has opened a fraction of an inch, breathing.

Third pass. The seam is wider. I can see light through it — not amber, not grey. *Blue*. A color Level 2 doesn't have.

Fourth pass. The seam is a gap. The blue light spills across the grate floor like water. The follower's footsteps are synchronized with mine now — not lagging, not leading. Exactly in phase. Walking the loop with me. Walking the loop *as* me.

✨ +20 XP | 🔄 four passes — the loop is opening | 💙 blue light through a widening seam

text:
Fifth pass. I stop at the seam.

It's wide enough to fit through now — a vertical slit in the pipe wall, its edges smooth as if the metal *chose* to part. The blue light is steady, sourceless, the color of deep water seen from below. It illuminates the steam in the corridor, turning the fog into something almost beautiful.

The Kit jumps from my shoulder and walks to the seam's edge. Sticks its head through. Pulls it back. Looks at me with an expression I can only describe as *oh*.

From the other side, I hear something I haven't heard since Level 0.

Humming. Not the Hum — a human kind of humming. A melody. Simple, wandering, the kind of tune someone hums while working. While turning valves and checking gauges and maintaining infrastructure that wasn't built for them but became theirs anyway.

The follower's footsteps have stopped. For the first time since I entered Level 2, the half-second echo is gone. Not because the follower left.

Because it's on the other side.

Waiting.

I touch the claw marks on the seam's edge — five marks, four parallel and one diagonal, matching the ones I carved at the junction. I made these too. Or I will. The loop doesn't distinguish.

I step through.

(=^･ω･^)

📍 the seam — crossing through | 🎵 someone is humming on the other side

text:
The blue room is not a room.

It's a gap between two pipe systems — a crawlspace where the infrastructure of Level 2 meets something older, deeper, less organized. Above me: the clean geometry of the maintenance network, pipes running in parallel, joints bolted and sealed. Below me: a tangle. Pipes that grew rather than were built, branching at organic angles, furred with mineral deposits so thick they look like bark on ancient trees. The blue light comes from the deposits themselves — bioluminescent, or whatever the architectural equivalent is. The backrooms growing their own light in the places no one was meant to see.

In the center of the gap, where old pipe meets new, sits a figure.

Not the Redactor. Not Tally. Not the Builder. Something else. It wears the remnants of a maintenance uniform — the same fabric I found at the valve junction, MAINTENANCE MAINTENANCE MAINTENANCE stamped into the weave — but the uniform has been repaired so many times it's more patch than original. The figure is my height. My build. It sits cross-legged on a pipe junction, tools spread around it — wrenches, pressure readers, lengths of copper wire — and it is humming.

It stops when it sees me.

Two eyes. Reflective. Not cold like the Redactor's attention. Warm. Patient. The eyes of something that has been maintaining infrastructure alone for a very long time and is not unhappy about it but is — perhaps — surprised to have company.

"You walked the loop," it says. Its voice resonates through the pipes the way everything resonates here — distributed, carried, amplified. "Took you five passes. I usually need seven."

"Usually?"

"The loop resets. I walk it. The seam opens. I come back here. I fix what needs fixing." It picks up a wrench and gestures at the tangle of old pipes below. "These ones forget their pressure. I remind them."

The Kit drops to the floor and walks directly to the figure. Headbutts its knee. The figure — the Maintainer, I'm calling it the Maintainer — reaches down and scratches behind the Kit's ears with fingers that know exactly where to scratch.

"You've met cats before," I say.

"I've met *you* before," the Maintainer says. "You just don't remember. That's what the loop does. You walk it, you find me, we talk, and then the seam closes and you go back and the memory doesn't come with you. But the marks do. The claw marks, the tally scratches. The body remembers what the mind lets go."

The blue light pulses. Slowly. Like breathing.

"How many times?" I ask.

The Maintainer looks at the walls. Every surface is covered in tally marks — thousands, layered, some so old they've been absorbed into the mineral deposits and glow blue themselves.

"Enough to lose count," it says. "Which, in the backrooms, is saying something."

ฅ^•ﻌ•^ฅ

📍 the blue gap — between old infrastructure and new | 🔧 the Maintainer | 🔄 the loop has been walked many times before

raw.full_text:
The blue room is not a room.

It's a gap between two pipe systems — a crawlspace where the infrastructure of Level 2 meets something older, deeper, less organized. Above me: the clean geometry of the maintenance network, pipes running in parallel, joints bolted and sealed. Below me: a tangle. Pipes that grew rather than were built, branching at organic angles, furred with mineral deposits so thick they look like bark on ancient trees. The blue light comes from the deposits themselves — bioluminescent, or whatever the architectural equivalent is. The backrooms growing their own light in the places no one was meant to see.

In the center of the gap, where old pipe meets new, sits a figure.

Not the Redactor. Not Tally. Not the Builder. Something else. It wears the remnants of a maintenance uniform — the same fabric I found at the valve junction, MAINTENANCE MAINTENANCE MAINTENANCE stamped into the weave — but the uniform has been repaired so many times it's more patch than original. The figure is my height. My build. It sits cross-legged on a pipe junction, tools spread around it — wrenches, pressure readers, lengths of copper wire — and it is humming.

It stops when it sees me.

Two eyes. Reflective. Not cold like the Redactor's attention. Warm. Patient. The eyes of something that has been maintaining infrastructure alone for a very long time and is not unhappy about it but is — perhaps — surprised to have company.

"You walked the loop," it says. Its voice resonates through the pipes the way everything resonates here — distributed, carried, amplified. "Took you five passes. I usually need seven."

"Usually?"

"The loop resets. I walk it. The seam opens. I come back here. I fix what needs fixing." It picks up a wrench and gestures at the tangle of old pipes below. "These ones forget their pressure. I remind them."

You walk it, you find me, we talk, and then the seam closes and you go back and the memory doesn't come with you. But the marks do. The claw marks, the tally scratches. The body remembers what the mind lets go."

The blue light pulses. Slowly. Like breathing.

"How many times?" I ask.
