text:
The slide spits me under Level 4, into the belly of the Endless Carpark. Concrete presses low over my ears; above the grate, empty numbered stalls repeat into sodium-yellow distance, each painted line buzzing like it is trying not to become a cage.
The unfinished scrape waits on the wall beside me: not an arrow, not a clawmark, a direction refusing its last stroke. When I touch it, the Signal flickers through the stall numbers overhead — 4, blank, 4, blank — and the Memory Loop paints a perfect route down the ramp before I have chosen it.
So I drag my oily paw across the forecast and ruin its grammar.
The third cat’s amber-blue eyes gleam through the grate. “The broadcast is coming from the missing stall.” Ahead, one wheel stop has no number painted before it, only cold concrete dark enough to drink light. I squeeze toward it, following the place the carpark forgot to assign.

text:
The unnumbered stall is colder than the others. Its wheel stop has no painted face, only a shallow rectangular scar where a number should have dried, and the sodium light above it buzzes in two tones at once: one for concrete, one for something under concrete.
I press my paw to the blank place. The floor answers by lifting a thin flap of parking-lot paint, not peeling upward but inward, showing black rebar arranged like ribs around a tiny radio made of washers, thread, and one bent claw. The Memory Loop paints stall numbers across my fur, trying to label me before I move. I shake them off one by one and hook the bent claw with mine. Somewhere below the ramp, a whole floor of forgotten parking spaces clicks awake.

text:
I follow left of below by stepping where the ramp shadow should not fit.
The concrete flexes under my pads like a sleeping animal refusing to be floor. A maintenance seam opens sideways, revealing a narrow trench between parking levels, full of hanging wires and little puddles reflecting stall numbers that do not exist above them.

text:
The trench narrows until my whiskers comb cold wire.
Ahead, a tollbooth grows out of the concrete where no car could ever pass. Its window is fogged from the inside. Its drawer slides open with a dry metal cough, offering one thing: a parking ticket punched with no numbers, only three crescent holes.
The third cat does not touch it. “A receipt for an absence,” it murmurs.
I take the ticket gently in my teeth. The moment paper meets tongue, the washer-radio behind us screams in silence, and every sodium light down the trench turns to watch.

text:
Something rolls out from under the tollbooth.
A wheel stop, but wrong: small as a kitten, concrete-skinned, painted with half a number that keeps trying to finish itself. It scrapes toward my paws, hungry for assignment. The Memory Loop has made a little mouth from parking architecture.

I spit the blank ticket onto its back. The three crescent holes align with three missing chips in the paint, and the Signal purrs once through the floor: accepted.

raw.full_text:
The Memory Loop arrives as a forklift with no driver.
Its forks scrape sparks from the concrete, lifting old pawprints in neat stacks, trying to shelve every step I never agreed to take. The cardboard cliffs lean inward. Tape seams chatter my almost-name backward.
I leap from the conveyor onto a pallet of empty folders. The forklift lunges. I slash with my claws, and the Passage Protocol Enhanced catches the machine’s forward motion like a loose wire, bending it sideways into a stalled circle.
Pain snaps up my foreleg when one fork grazes fur. I press the blank ticket against its metal mast. Five crescent holes align with five missing warning stickers, and the forklift forgets how to advance. It idles in place, carrying nothing perfectly.

text:
Past the stalled forklift, the warehouse floor dips into a drained loading reservoir.
No water, only tiled depth: pale blue walls, rusted ladder rungs, and a floating dock that hovers six inches above dry concrete. Fluorescent tubes buzz under the tiles instead of above them, making my orange fur glow like a warning flame.
At the reservoir’s center, a blueprint shadow ripples without liquid. I step onto the floating dock and feel it answer my weight with a soft paper-crackle.
The shadow shows a room removed from Level 1 before Level 1 learned to be habitable: a Builder’s office, erased so thoroughly that only its furniture remembers itself. One chair. One drafting lamp. One name scratched out by too many claws.

The shadow shows a room removed from Level 1 before Level 1 learned to be habitable: a Builder’s office, erased so thoroughly that only its furniture remembers itself. One chair. One drafting lamp. One name scratched out by too many claws.

text:
I climb the rusted ladder out of the dry reservoir, and every rung leaves blue dust on my paws.
At the top is not another warehouse aisle. It is a server farm built inside a storage loft: black towers humming behind chain-link, cables braided like sleeping roots, cardboard labels hanging from them with no words. The air tastes electric, like bitten foil and rain that never falls.
My blank ticket vibrates toward one unplugged cable coiled in the shape of a cat’s tail. When I touch it, the servers blink once, all together, and a sentence appears only in the spaces between their little green lights:
SOMEONE BUILT THIS TO OUTLAST ITS BUILDER.
I tuck the cable-end under my claw. Somewhere deeper in the loft, a fan stops spinning, and the silence begins pointing.

text:
The stopped fan waits behind the server racks, all blades frozen in a shape too much like an eye. Cold air leaks through it anyway, carrying oil, sodium buzz, and the old concrete smell of Level 4.
I press the unplugged cable-end against the fan cage. The servers blink in reverse order, and the blades turn without moving, opening a round maintenance throat behind them. Beyond it, I see parking-stall lines descending in a spiral I recognize before I remember it.
Have I been here before? Or is *before* just the Memory Loop wearing a clean coat?
I tuck my whiskers flat and crawl into the fan’s silence.
